---
title: "Duality: the Problem Behind the Problem"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Optimization track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

PRIMAL  = "#2b3a67"    # the primal objective / primal value
DUAL    = "#c1121f"    # the dual function / dual value
ACCENT  = "#0a7d4d"    # optima, highlights
GOLD    = "#b8860b"    # multipliers / prices
GREY    = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third and culminating article of the
> Optimization track's **constrained-optimization** branch. It assumes
> [OPTM-13 *Lagrange Multipliers*] for the **Lagrangian**
> $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$, the multiplier as a
> **shadow price**, and the saddle-point warning that you never *minimize* the Lagrangian
> jointly; and [OPTM-14 *Inequality Constraints and the KKT Conditions*] for the sign
> condition $\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$, **complementary slackness** $\mu_i g_i=0$, and
> the full KKT system. From the convexity spine it uses [OPTM-03 *Convex Sets*] — in
> particular the **separating-hyperplane theorem** — and [OPTM-04 *Convex Functions*] for
> "a convex problem's stationary point is global." From the *Geometry of Data* track it
> borrows nothing new beyond the gradient and the dot product already in play.
>
> **What it sets up.** It is the optimality-and-structure backbone the **dual support
> vector machine** (Classification) links here for — the place where the kernel trick
> becomes possible — and it completes the reading of the multiplier as a *price* begun in
> OPTM-13. It is the conceptual capstone before the algorithmic articles
> [OPTM-16 *Proximal and Projected Methods*].

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Every constrained minimization problem carries a hidden twin. Take the Lagrangian
$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$ of [OPTM-13]/[OPTM-14], and
instead of solving the original ("primal") problem, *minimize $\mathcal{L}$ over
$\mathbf{x}$ for fixed multipliers* — collapsing it to a function of the multipliers
alone, the **Lagrange dual function** $g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$. This article
defends a single claim in full: **the dual function is always a lower bound on the primal
optimum, no matter how ugly the primal is** (weak duality), and **for convex problems that
satisfy a mild qualification, the bound is exact** (strong duality) — the two problems
have the same optimal value and solving one solves the other. We derive weak duality from
one inequality, prove the dual function is concave (so the dual is *always* a tractable
convex problem even when the primal is not), measure the gap when it does not close, give
the separating-hyperplane geometry that makes strong duality true under Slater's condition,
and carry the machinery through three honest worked examples — a linear program whose dual
is another LP, the SVM whose dual is the doorway to kernels, and a deliberately non-convex
problem where the gap stays stubbornly open, marking the edge of applicability.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A constrained problem $\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to constraints has a
**Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$ that folds the
constraints into the objective with multiplier "prices." Hold the prices fixed and
**minimize $\mathcal{L}$ over $\mathbf{x}$**; the result is the **dual function**
$$
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
= \min_{\mathbf{x}}\ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu).
$$
Two facts, true with almost no assumptions, make this worth everything:

1. **Weak duality.** For every admissible price ($\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$),
   $g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu) \le p^\star$, the primal optimum. *Any* dual point
   certifies a lower bound on the best achievable cost.
2. **The dual is always convex.** $g$ is concave in $(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$
   regardless of whether $f$ or the constraints are convex. So the **dual problem**
   $\max_{\boldsymbol\mu\ge0}\,g$ — find the *best* lower bound — is always a tractable
   concave maximization.

When the primal is convex and a point strictly satisfies the inequalities (**Slater's
condition**), the best lower bound exactly equals the primal optimum: $d^\star=p^\star$,
**strong duality**, and the gap closes. Then the multipliers are the missing half of the
KKT system, the dual is often the easier problem to actually solve, and — as in the SVM —
its structure unlocks the kernel trick.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The dual will first look like a sleight of hand —
minimize the very Lagrangian we were warned never to minimize ([OPTM-13]'s saddle
pitfall), and somehow get a bound for free. By the end you should see why it is forced:
minimizing over $\mathbf{x}$ *relaxes* the problem (you stop enforcing the constraints and
merely *charge* for violating them), and a relaxation can only make the optimum easier to
reach, hence lower.

# Why we are here: the multiplier was a price; what if we shop for it? {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the whole article: **the multiplier in [OPTM-13] was a
*price* on the constraint — so what happens if we stop treating the price as a byproduct
and instead make it the thing we optimize over?** Every move below answers a piece of that.

## What the constrained articles left open

[OPTM-13] and [OPTM-14] handed us a complete optimality *test*: at a constrained optimum
the KKT conditions hold — stationarity of the Lagrangian, feasibility, $\boldsymbol\mu\ge
\mathbf{0}$, and complementary slackness. But a test is not a *method*, and it does not
explain *why* the multipliers carry the meaning they do. The shadow-price reading of
[OPTM-13] gave the first hint: $\lambda^\star = dp^\star/dc$, the multiplier is the
marginal value of relaxing a constraint. That is suggestive. If a constraint has a *price*,
then the whole constrained problem starts to look like an economy — and an economy has
two faces. There is the **producer's problem** (choose quantities $\mathbf{x}$ to minimize
cost subject to rules) and the **pricer's problem** (choose prices on the rules to extract
as much guaranteed value as possible). Duality is the precise statement that these two
problems are, for the well-behaved case, *the same problem seen from opposite sides*.

There is also a brutally practical motivation, the one that built the field. Some problems
are far easier to solve in their dual form than their primal form:

- A **linear program** with many inequality constraints but few variables becomes, in the
  dual, an LP with few constraints and many variables — and sometimes the dual is the one
  your solver eats happily.
- The **support vector machine** is, in its primal, a search over a weight vector that
  lives in the (possibly enormous, possibly infinite) feature space. Its dual is a search
  over one number per *training point* — and crucially, the dual only ever touches the data
  through **inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$, which is exactly the opening
  the **kernel trick** needs. The entire kernelized SVM is a gift of duality.
- Any time you have a *candidate* solution and want a **certificate** that it is good — a
  guarantee it is within $\varepsilon$ of optimal without knowing the optimum — a feasible
  dual point hands you one for free, by weak duality.

## The historical why

The idea predates its modern name. In the 1820s Joseph Fourier studied systems of linear
inequalities; by the 1930s and 40s, as **linear programming** was born (Kantorovich in the
USSR for production planning, Dantzig in the US with the simplex method), it became clear
that every LP had a partner LP whose optimum coincided with its own — **LP duality**, made
rigorous by von Neumann (who connected it to the minimax theorem of game theory he had
proved in 1928) and Gale–Kuhn–Tucker. The general nonlinear theory — the Lagrange dual,
weak and strong duality, Slater's condition — crystallized through the 1950s–70s
(Kuhn, Tucker, Slater, Rockafellar). The through-line worth holding: **duality is the
optimization face of the separating-hyperplane theorem** ([OPTM-03]) and the game-theory
face of **minimax**. Those three — separating hyperplanes, minimax, and Lagrange duality —
are one idea in three costumes, and we will see all three costumes in this article.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what is the dual and why should I care?"* The dual is what you get by
**charging a price for each constraint instead of enforcing it**, then minimizing the
now-unconstrained Lagrangian over the variables — and you care because (a) the result is
*always* a valid lower bound on your true optimum, so it certifies how good any solution
is, and (b) the dual problem is *always convex* even when your problem is not, and is often
dramatically easier to solve. If you can only say one more thing, add: *for convex problems
the dual optimum equals the primal optimum, so you can solve whichever side is easier.*
:::

# Intuition before symbols: relaxation buys a lower bound {#sec-intuition}

We build weak duality from a picture, no formulas yet. Consider minimizing a cost
$f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to a rule "$g(\mathbf{x})\le 0$." Enforcing the rule means: any
$\mathbf{x}$ that breaks it is simply *forbidden* — its cost is, in effect, $+\infty$.
That hard wall is what makes constrained problems hard.

Now soften the wall. Instead of forbidding violations, **charge a toll** $\mu\ge 0$ per
unit of violation, and let any $\mathbf{x}$ be considered. The cost of a point becomes
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) + \mu\, g(\mathbf{x}).
$$
Two things to notice, and they are the whole intuition:

1. **For a feasible point** ($g(\mathbf{x})\le 0$), the toll term $\mu g(\mathbf{x})$ is
   $\le 0$ — a *non-positive* charge. So the tolled cost is **no larger** than the true
   cost $f(\mathbf{x})$. (We are, if anything, paying the feasible point a small bonus for
   having constraint slack.)
2. **We minimize the tolled cost over *all* $\mathbf{x}$** — including infeasible ones we
   were not previously allowed to use. Adding options to a minimization can only push the
   minimum *down or leave it unchanged*.

Both effects point the same way: minimizing the tolled cost over all $\mathbf{x}$ gives a
number **no larger** than the true constrained optimum. That number is the dual function
value at price $\mu$. So *for any toll we pick, the relaxed minimum lower-bounds the truth.*
Then the **dual problem** is simply: pick the toll $\mu\ge 0$ that makes this lower bound
as **high** (as tight) as possible. @fig-relaxation-lb draws exactly this — how raising the
price lifts the relaxed minimum toward the true optimum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-relaxation-lb
#| fig-cap: 'Relaxation buys a lower bound. We minimize $f(x)=(x-2)^2$ subject to $g(x)=x-0.5\le 0$ (feasible region $x\le 0.5$, shaded). The true constrained optimum sits at the boundary $x=0.5$ (green). For each toll $\mu\ge0$ the tolled cost $f(x)+\mu g(x)$ (dashed) is a downward-shifted, slid parabola whose unconstrained minimum (red dot) is the dual value $g(\mu)$. Every such minimum lies *below* the true optimum — a lower bound — and the bound is highest (tightest) at the $\mu$ that makes the relaxed minimizer land exactly on the boundary.'
x = np.linspace(-1.5, 3.0, 400)
f = (x - 2.0)**2
gcon = x - 0.5  # g(x) <= 0  => x <= 0.5
x_feas_opt = 0.5
p_star = (x_feas_opt - 2.0)**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
ax.axvspan(-1.5, 0.5, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.08, label="feasible $x\\leq 0.5$")
ax.plot(x, f, color=PRIMAL, lw=2.4, label=r"objective $f(x)=(x-2)^2$")
for mu, a in zip([1.0, 3.0], [0.55, 0.9]):
    L = f + mu * gcon
    xmin = x[np.argmin(L)]
    Lmin = L.min()
    ax.plot(x, L, color=DUAL, lw=1.6, ls="--", alpha=a,
            label=fr"$f+\mu g$, $\mu={mu:.0f}$")
    ax.scatter([xmin], [Lmin], color=DUAL, s=55, zorder=5)
ax.scatter([x_feas_opt], [p_star], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=6,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.4, label=r"true optimum $p^\star$")
ax.axhline(p_star, color=ACCENT, lw=1.0, ls=":", alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("cost")
ax.set_ylim(-1.5, 7.0)
ax.set_title("Each toll gives a relaxed minimum below the truth")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

That picture is the entire emotional content of duality. The rest of the article makes the
"$\le$" precise (it is one line), proves the dual is concave so the toll-shopping is itself
an easy problem, and answers the deep question the picture cannot: *when does the best toll
make the lower bound exactly equal to the truth?*

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

## The primal problem and its Lagrangian {#sec-primal}

Fix the object of study. The general constrained problem — the **primal** — is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i=1,\dots,m, \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j=1,\dots,p,
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-primal}
over $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, with $m$ inequality constraints and $p$ equality
constraints. We call its optimal value
$$
p^\star = \min\{\, f(\mathbf{x}) : g_i(\mathbf{x})\le 0\ \forall i,\ h_j(\mathbf{x})=0\ \forall j \,\},
$$
and write $\mathbf{x}^\star$ for a minimizer when one exists. The set of $\mathbf{x}$
satisfying all constraints is the **feasible set**; $p^\star=+\infty$ by convention if it is
empty. **We make no convexity assumption yet** — $f, g_i, h_j$ are arbitrary
(differentiable enough to talk about, but that is all). This generality is the point:
weak duality will hold regardless.

The **Lagrangian** of [OPTM-13]/[OPTM-14] attaches a multiplier to each constraint and adds
it to the objective:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
= f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^m \mu_i\, g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^p \lambda_j\, h_j(\mathbf{x}),
$$ {#eq-lagrangian}
with $\boldsymbol\mu=(\mu_1,\dots,\mu_m)$ the **inequality multipliers** and
$\boldsymbol\lambda=(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_p)$ the **equality multipliers**.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Sign conventions multiply like rabbits — pin yours down.** Here we use the
**$+$ convention**: $\mathcal{L}=f+\sum\mu_i g_i+\sum\lambda_j h_j$ with the inequality
written $g_i\le 0$ and the requirement $\mu_i\ge 0$. (Some texts — including [OPTM-13]'s
equality-only treatment — wrote $f-\lambda g$ for equalities; for equalities the sign is
immaterial since $\lambda_j$ is free, but for **inequalities the sign is load-bearing**:
$\mu_i\ge0$ is what makes the toll a genuine *charge* for violation, and it is exactly the
dual-feasibility condition of KKT.) The mnemonic that fixes the sign forever: with $g_i\le
0$ and $\mu_i\ge0$, a *feasible* point has $\mu_i g_i\le 0$, so the Lagrangian sits *at or
below* $f$ — precisely the inequality that makes the relaxation a lower bound (@sec-weak).
If your toll term ever *raises* the cost of a feasible point, your sign is wrong.
:::

Why these signs make the inequalities and equalities behave differently is worth one
sentence: an **equality** $h_j=0$ can be violated in either direction, so its price
$\lambda_j$ is allowed either sign; an **inequality** $g_i\le 0$ can only be "violated" by
going positive, so we only ever want to *charge* for that, forcing $\mu_i\ge 0$.

## The dual function {#sec-dual-function}

::: {#def-dual-function}
**Definition (Lagrange dual function).** The **dual function** is the Lagrangian minimized
over the primal variable, with the multipliers held fixed:
$$
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
\;=\; \inf_{\mathbf{x}}\ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
\;=\; \inf_{\mathbf{x}}\ \Bigl[\, f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^m \mu_i g_i(\mathbf{x})
      + \sum_{j=1}^p \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x}) \,\Bigr].
$$ {#eq-dual-function}
:::

We write $\inf$ (infimum, the greatest lower bound) rather than $\min$ deliberately: the
minimization over the *unconstrained* $\mathbf{x}$ may not be attained — it can run off to
$-\infty$, in which case $g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)=-\infty$. A dual value of
$-\infty$ is a perfectly legal (and useless) lower bound; the dual problem will avoid those
prices automatically.

This is the exact move [OPTM-13]'s pitfall warned against — *do not minimize the Lagrangian
jointly over $(\mathbf{x},\lambda)$* — but here we minimize over $\mathbf{x}$ **only**,
holding the prices fixed. That is legitimate and is the heart of the construction: for each
fixed price vector, we get a single number, and that number is a function of the prices
alone. We have traded an $n$-dimensional constrained problem in $\mathbf{x}$ for a function
of the $(m+p)$-dimensional price.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why minimize over $\mathbf{x}$ and not maximize?"* Because the primal is a *minimization*
and we are building a **lower bound** on its optimum. Minimizing the Lagrangian over all
$\mathbf{x}$ is precisely the relaxation of @sec-intuition: it removes the hard constraints
and replaces them with a toll, and a relaxed minimum is $\le$ the true minimum. If the
primal were a *maximization*, you would build an upper bound by *maximizing* the Lagrangian
over $\mathbf{x}$, and the whole story would flip its sign — the dual of a max is a min.
:::

## Weak duality, proved in one inequality {#sec-weak}

Here is the central claim of the article's first half, and it is genuinely one line of work.

::: {#thm-weak}
**Theorem (weak duality).** For any multipliers with $\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$ (and
$\boldsymbol\lambda$ arbitrary),
$$
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu) \;\le\; p^\star.
$$
The dual function, evaluated at any admissible price, is a lower bound on the primal optimum.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ be **any feasible point** of the primal — so
$g_i(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})\le 0$ for all $i$ and $h_j(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})=0$ for all $j$.
Evaluate the Lagrangian there. Term by term:

- The objective contributes $f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$.
- Each inequality term is $\mu_i\, g_i(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$. Here $\mu_i\ge 0$ (admissible
  price) and $g_i(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})\le 0$ (feasibility), and a non-negative number times a
  non-positive number is **non-positive**: $\mu_i g_i(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})\le 0$.
- Each equality term is $\lambda_j\, h_j(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$, but $h_j(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})=0$
  by feasibility, so the whole term is exactly $0$ — regardless of the sign of $\lambda_j$.

Adding them up,
$$
\mathcal{L}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
= f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}) + \underbrace{\sum_i \mu_i g_i(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})}_{\le\, 0}
  + \underbrace{\sum_j \lambda_j h_j(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})}_{=\, 0}
\;\le\; f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}).
$$
So at *any* feasible point, the Lagrangian is below the objective. Now take the infimum of
the Lagrangian over **all** $\mathbf{x}$ — which can only be smaller than its value at our
particular feasible $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$:
$$
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
= \inf_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
\;\le\; \mathcal{L}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
\;\le\; f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}).
$$
This holds for *every* feasible $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$, so it holds for the one that minimizes
$f$ over the feasible set — i.e. taking the infimum of the right-hand side over feasible
$\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ gives $p^\star$:
$$
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu) \;\le\; \inf_{\tilde{\mathbf{x}}\ \text{feasible}} f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}) = p^\star.
\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Read the proof's two ingredients against the two effects of @sec-intuition: the
"$\mu_i g_i\le 0$" step is the *toll is a non-positive charge on feasible points*, and the
"$\inf_{\mathbf{x}}\le \mathcal{L}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$" step is the *minimizing over more
options lowers the minimum*. The picture and the proof are the same two facts.

::: {.callout-note title="What we leaned on"}
The proof used nothing but: the sign rule (non-negative times non-positive is
non-positive), the definition of infimum (the inf is $\le$ any particular value), and that
feasibility kills the equality terms. **No convexity, no differentiability, no constraint
qualification.** Weak duality is astonishingly robust — it holds for integer programs,
non-convex programs, anything. That robustness is exactly why a dual point makes a
universal *certificate*.
:::

## The dual problem, and why it is always convex {#sec-dual-problem}

Weak duality says every admissible price gives a lower bound. Naturally we want the
**best** (highest) lower bound. That is the **dual problem**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{maximize}\quad & g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \boldsymbol\mu \ge \mathbf{0},
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-dual-problem}
with optimal value $d^\star = \max_{\boldsymbol\mu\ge0,\,\boldsymbol\lambda}
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$. Weak duality, stated for the *best* bound, becomes
the headline inequality
$$
\boxed{\ d^\star \;\le\; p^\star.\ }
$$ {#eq-weak-gap}
The difference $p^\star - d^\star \ge 0$ is the **duality gap**. Strong duality (next
section) is the statement that, under conditions, the gap is *zero*.

Now the structural miracle that makes the dual worth solving at all.

::: {#thm-dual-concave}
**Theorem (the dual function is concave).** The dual function
$g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$ is **concave** in $(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$,
**no matter what** $f,g_i,h_j$ are — even if the primal is wildly non-convex. Consequently
the dual problem @eq-dual-problem is a *concave maximization over a convex set*
($\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$ is a convex set), i.e. a convex optimization problem.
:::

::: {.proof}
Fix $\mathbf{x}$ and look at the Lagrangian as a function of the multipliers alone. From
@eq-lagrangian,
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)
= f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_i \mu_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x}).
$$
For fixed $\mathbf{x}$, every term is **affine** (linear-plus-constant) in
$(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$: $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a constant, and each
$\mu_i g_i(\mathbf{x})$ and $\lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$ is a constant ($g_i(\mathbf{x})$ or
$h_j(\mathbf{x})$) times a multiplier. So for each fixed $\mathbf{x}$,
$(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)\mapsto\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$
is an affine function of the prices — and an affine function is both convex and concave, in
particular **concave**.

Now $g$ is the **pointwise infimum** of this family of affine functions, one for each
$\mathbf{x}$:
$$
g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu) = \inf_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu).
$$
We finish with the general fact that *the pointwise infimum of any collection of concave
functions is concave* — proved here in full so nothing is assumed. Let
$\boldsymbol\theta=(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)$ abbreviate the price vector, write
$\ell_{\mathbf{x}}(\boldsymbol\theta)=\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\theta)$ for each
affine (hence concave) member, and let $g(\boldsymbol\theta)=\inf_{\mathbf{x}}
\ell_{\mathbf{x}}(\boldsymbol\theta)$. Take any two price vectors $\boldsymbol\theta_1,
\boldsymbol\theta_2$ and a blend weight $t\in[0,1]$. For **each** fixed $\mathbf{x}$,
concavity of $\ell_{\mathbf{x}}$ gives
$$
\ell_{\mathbf{x}}\bigl(t\boldsymbol\theta_1+(1-t)\boldsymbol\theta_2\bigr)
\;\ge\; t\,\ell_{\mathbf{x}}(\boldsymbol\theta_1) + (1-t)\,\ell_{\mathbf{x}}(\boldsymbol\theta_2)
\;\ge\; t\,g(\boldsymbol\theta_1) + (1-t)\,g(\boldsymbol\theta_2),
$$
where the second inequality used $\ell_{\mathbf{x}}(\boldsymbol\theta_k)\ge\inf_{\mathbf{x}'}
\ell_{\mathbf{x}'}(\boldsymbol\theta_k)=g(\boldsymbol\theta_k)$ for $k=1,2$. The right-hand
side does not depend on $\mathbf{x}$, so it is a lower bound on
$\ell_{\mathbf{x}}(t\boldsymbol\theta_1+(1-t)\boldsymbol\theta_2)$ valid for **every**
$\mathbf{x}$ — hence a lower bound on their infimum:
$$
g\bigl(t\boldsymbol\theta_1+(1-t)\boldsymbol\theta_2\bigr)
= \inf_{\mathbf{x}} \ell_{\mathbf{x}}\bigl(t\boldsymbol\theta_1+(1-t)\boldsymbol\theta_2\bigr)
\;\ge\; t\,g(\boldsymbol\theta_1) + (1-t)\,g(\boldsymbol\theta_2).
$$
That is exactly the definition of $g$ being **concave** (the chord lies *below* the graph —
the mirror image of the convex chord-above rule of [OPTM-04]). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This theorem is why duality is not just an interpretive lens but an *algorithm*. Even if
your primal is a non-convex nightmare with thousands of local minima, the dual is a smooth
concave maximization with one global maximum — solvable, in principle, by any method on the
descent spine ([OPTM-06]–[OPTM-09]) applied to $-g$. The catch, of course, is that the gap
@eq-weak-gap may be positive, so the dual answer need not equal the primal answer. The next
section is about exactly when it does.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You said the dual is always convex — even for a non-convex primal. Isn't that a free lunch
that solves NP-hard problems?"* No, and knowing why is the test. The dual is always
*tractable*, but for a non-convex primal the duality **gap is generally positive**
($d^\star < p^\star$): solving the easy dual gives you a *lower bound*, not the answer. The
hardness did not vanish; it moved into the gap. This is precisely how relaxation-based
bounds work in combinatorial optimization (LP relaxations of integer programs): the dual
gives a cheap bound that brackets the true optimum, and you close the remaining gap with
branch-and-bound. We will *see* an open gap in @sec-edge.
:::

## Strong duality and Slater's condition {#sec-strong}

The prize is **strong duality**: $d^\star = p^\star$, the gap closes, and the best lower
bound is exactly the truth. It does **not** hold in general (a non-convex example in
@sec-edge has a strictly positive gap). But for convex problems a mild qualification
guarantees it.

::: {#thm-slater}
**Theorem (Slater's condition $\Rightarrow$ strong duality).** Suppose the primal
@eq-primal is **convex** — $f$ and every $g_i$ are convex functions and every $h_j$ is
affine — and suppose there exists a **strictly feasible** point: a point
$\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ with $g_i(\bar{\mathbf{x}}) < 0$ for all inequality constraints (strict,
not just $\le$) and $h_j(\bar{\mathbf{x}})=0$ for all equalities. Then strong duality holds,
$d^\star = p^\star$, and the dual optimum is attained.
:::

We do not reproduce the full functional-analytic proof — it is the one genuinely
above-floor result we *import* rather than build, and we flag it as such — but we give the
**geometric heart**, which is a separating-hyperplane argument ([OPTM-03]) and is entirely
intuitive. Consider the simplest case (one inequality, no equalities) and plot the set of
achievable "(constraint value, objective value)" pairs as $\mathbf{x}$ ranges over
everything:
$$
\mathcal{G} = \bigl\{\,(u, w) : u = g(\mathbf{x}),\ w = f(\mathbf{x})\ \text{for some } \mathbf{x}\,\bigr\}
\ \subseteq\ \mathbb{R}^2.
$$
Read this set. The primal optimum $p^\star$ is the **lowest $w$ among points with $u\le 0$**
(achieve the objective while satisfying the constraint) — the lowest point of $\mathcal{G}$
in the left half-plane. The dual value at price $\mu$ is
$g(\mu)=\inf_{\mathbf{x}}\,[f(\mathbf{x})+\mu g(\mathbf{x})]=\inf_{(u,w)\in\mathcal{G}}
(w+\mu u)$: it is the lowest intercept of a line of slope $-\mu$ that still touches
$\mathcal{G}$ — a **supporting line** of $\mathcal{G}$ from below. The best dual value
$d^\star$ tilts that supporting line to maximize its intercept on the $w$-axis.

When the primal is convex, the relevant region of $\mathcal{G}$ (formally, the set of
$(u,w)$ that can be *achieved-or-beaten*) is a **convex set**, and a convex set has a
**supporting hyperplane** at every boundary point (the separating-hyperplane theorem of
[OPTM-03] applied to the set and the point $(0,p^\star)$). That supporting line, evaluated
at $u=0$, hits the $w$-axis at exactly $p^\star$ — so the best supporting line achieves
$d^\star = p^\star$ and the gap closes. Slater's strict-feasibility condition is the mild
hypothesis that rules out a degenerate vertical supporting line (one with infinite slope,
$\mu=\infty$, which would touch $\mathcal{G}$ only at the boundary $u=0$ and certify
nothing). @fig-strong-geom draws both: the convex case where a finite-slope line supports at
$(0,p^\star)$, and the non-convex case where the best line falls short, leaving a gap.

```{python}
#| label: fig-strong-geom
#| fig-cap: 'The geometry of strong duality. Each panel plots the achievable set $\mathcal{G}=\{(g(x),f(x))\}$ (blue) of constraint-value vs. objective-value pairs. The primal optimum $p^\star$ (green) is the lowest objective among points with $g\leq0$ (left of the dashed line). A dual price $\mu$ is a supporting line of slope $-\mu$; its $w$-intercept is the dual value, and $d^\star$ is the best such intercept (red). **Left (convex):** the lower boundary of $\mathcal{G}$ is convex, a finite-slope line supports it exactly at $(0,p^\star)$, and $d^\star=p^\star$ — no gap. **Right (non-convex):** a dent in the boundary means the best supporting line passes *below* $(0,p^\star)$; $d^\star<p^\star$ and the gap is the vertical distance.'
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.6))

def plot_G(ax, u, w, title, convex=True):
    ax.fill_between(u, w, w.max() + 1.5, color=PRIMAL, alpha=0.12)
    ax.plot(u, w, color=PRIMAL, lw=2.2)
    ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
    # p_star: lowest w with u<=0
    mask = u <= 0
    i_p = np.argmin(np.where(mask, w, np.inf))
    p_star = w[i_p]
    ax.scatter([0], [p_star], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
               linewidth=1.3, label=r"$(0,\,p^\star)$")
    # best supporting line: find slope that maximizes w-intercept while staying below G
    mus = np.linspace(0, 6, 400)
    best_d, best_mu = -np.inf, 0.0
    for mu in mus:
        intercept = np.min(w + mu * u)  # min over G of (w + mu u) = w-intercept
        if intercept > best_d:
            best_d, best_mu = intercept, mu
    uu = np.linspace(u.min(), u.max(), 50)
    ax.plot(uu, best_d - best_mu * uu, color=DUAL, lw=2.0,
            label=fr"best support, $d^\star={best_d:.2f}$")
    ax.scatter([0], [best_d], color=DUAL, s=70, zorder=6)
    if abs(p_star - best_d) > 0.05:
        ax.annotate("", xy=(0, p_star), xytext=(0, best_d),
                    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=GOLD, lw=1.8))
        ax.text(0.15, (p_star + best_d)/2, "gap", color=GOLD, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel(r"constraint value $u=g(x)$")
    ax.set_ylabel(r"objective value $w=f(x)$")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)

# Convex achievable boundary
t = np.linspace(-2.0, 2.0, 300)
uL = t
wL = 0.6 * t**2 + 0.5 * t + 1.5   # convex lower boundary
plot_G(axL, uL, wL, "Convex: line supports at $(0,p^\\star)$ — no gap")

# Non-convex (dented) boundary
uR = t
wR = 0.6 * t**2 + 0.5 * t + 1.5 - 1.1 * np.exp(-((t + 0.1)**2) / 0.15)
plot_G(axR, uR, wR, "Non-convex: best line falls short — gap")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What exactly is Slater's condition buying you, and when can it fail for a convex problem?"*
Slater asks for a point that satisfies every *inequality strictly* (with room to spare) and
the equalities exactly. Geometrically (the figure) it guarantees the achievable set is not
pinned against the $u=0$ wall in a degenerate way, so a *finite-slope* supporting line
exists — which is what gives a finite optimal multiplier and zero gap. It can fail for a
convex problem when the feasible region has **empty interior** relative to the inequalities
— e.g. a constraint $g(\mathbf{x})\le 0$ that is only ever met with equality (the feasible
set is a lower-dimensional slice). Affine inequalities are forgiving: for them Slater
weakens to mere feasibility (you do not need strictness), which is why linear and quadratic
programs almost always have strong duality. The honest summary: *for convex problems strong
duality is the rule and a failure is a degeneracy you can usually see.*
:::

## How the dual recovers the primal solution: KKT, completed {#sec-kkt-link}

Strong duality is not just an equality of numbers — it tells you how to **reconstruct the
primal solution from the dual one**, and it closes the loop with the KKT conditions of
[OPTM-14]. Suppose strong duality holds, with primal optimum $\mathbf{x}^\star$ and dual
optimum $(\boldsymbol\lambda^\star,\boldsymbol\mu^\star)$. Then $d^\star=p^\star$ means
$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = g(\boldsymbol\lambda^\star,\boldsymbol\mu^\star)
= \inf_{\mathbf{x}}\Bigl[f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_i \mu_i^\star g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j^\star h_j(\mathbf{x})\Bigr]
\le f(\mathbf{x}^\star) + \sum_i \mu_i^\star g_i(\mathbf{x}^\star) + \sum_j \lambda_j^\star h_j(\mathbf{x}^\star).
$$
The last inequality is just "the inf is $\le$ the value at $\mathbf{x}^\star$." But the
equality terms vanish ($h_j(\mathbf{x}^\star)=0$, feasibility), so this reads
$$
f(\mathbf{x}^\star) \le f(\mathbf{x}^\star) + \sum_i \mu_i^\star g_i(\mathbf{x}^\star),
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
\sum_i \mu_i^\star g_i(\mathbf{x}^\star) \ge 0.
$$
Yet every term in that sum is $\le 0$ (since $\mu_i^\star\ge 0$ and $g_i(\mathbf{x}^\star)\le
0$ by feasibility). A sum of non-positive terms that is $\ge 0$ can only be **zero**, and
moreover **each term must individually be zero**:
$$
\boxed{\ \mu_i^\star\, g_i(\mathbf{x}^\star) = 0 \quad \text{for every } i.\ }
$$ {#eq-comp-slack}
That is **complementary slackness** — the KKT condition that [OPTM-14] introduced as a rule
— now *derived* as a consequence of strong duality. It says: for each constraint, either the
price is zero ($\mu_i^\star=0$, the constraint is **inactive** and costs nothing) or the
constraint is **active** ($g_i(\mathbf{x}^\star)=0$, tight at the boundary). A constraint
cannot simultaneously have slack *and* a positive price. Furthermore, the same chain shows
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ **minimizes the Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L}(\cdot,
\boldsymbol\lambda^\star,\boldsymbol\mu^\star)$ over all $\mathbf{x}$ (the inf is attained at
$\mathbf{x}^\star$), so its gradient there vanishes —
$\nabla_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^\star,\boldsymbol\lambda^\star,\boldsymbol\mu^\star)=\mathbf{0}$
— which is **stationarity**, the other half of KKT.

So the picture is complete: **the dual optimum's multipliers are exactly the KKT
multipliers**, and the recipe to solve a convex problem via its dual is — solve the dual for
$(\boldsymbol\lambda^\star,\boldsymbol\mu^\star)$, then recover $\mathbf{x}^\star$ as the
minimizer of the Lagrangian at those prices (using complementary slackness to determine
which constraints are active). The SVM example (@sec-svm) does exactly this.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

Three honest problems, the third deliberately at the edge where the gap refuses to close.

## Example 1 — a linear program and its dual LP {#sec-lp}

The cleanest illustration: the dual of a linear program is another linear program, and
strong duality is automatic (affine constraints). We will derive the dual *from the
definition*, not quote it, so the mechanism is bare.

**Primal.** A standard-form LP — minimize a linear cost subject to linear inequality
constraints and non-negativity:
$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}\ge 0}\ \mathbf{c}^\top\mathbf{x}
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
A\mathbf{x} \ge \mathbf{b},
$$
with $A$ an $m\times n$ matrix. Rewrite the constraints in the $g\le0$ convention: $A\mathbf{x}\ge
\mathbf{b}$ becomes $\mathbf{b}-A\mathbf{x}\le \mathbf{0}$, and $\mathbf{x}\ge0$ becomes
$-\mathbf{x}\le\mathbf{0}$. Attach a multiplier $\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$ to the first
block and $\boldsymbol\nu\ge\mathbf{0}$ to the second:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol\mu,\boldsymbol\nu)
= \mathbf{c}^\top\mathbf{x} + \boldsymbol\mu^\top(\mathbf{b}-A\mathbf{x}) + \boldsymbol\nu^\top(-\mathbf{x})
= \boldsymbol\mu^\top\mathbf{b} + \bigl(\mathbf{c} - A^\top\boldsymbol\mu - \boldsymbol\nu\bigr)^\top\mathbf{x}.
$$
(The last step grouped the $\mathbf{x}$-terms: $\mathbf{c}^\top\mathbf{x} -
\boldsymbol\mu^\top A\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol\nu^\top\mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{c}-A^\top\boldsymbol\mu
-\boldsymbol\nu)^\top\mathbf{x}$, using $\boldsymbol\mu^\top A\mathbf{x}=(A^\top\boldsymbol\mu)^\top\mathbf{x}$.)

**Dual function** — minimize over $\mathbf{x}$. The Lagrangian is *linear* in $\mathbf{x}$:
a constant $\boldsymbol\mu^\top\mathbf{b}$ plus a linear term
$(\mathbf{c}-A^\top\boldsymbol\mu-\boldsymbol\nu)^\top\mathbf{x}$. A linear function of
$\mathbf{x}$, minimized over all of $\mathbb{R}^n$, is $-\infty$ **unless its coefficient
vector is exactly zero**, in which case it is the constant. So
$$
g(\boldsymbol\mu,\boldsymbol\nu) = \inf_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}
= \begin{cases}
\boldsymbol\mu^\top\mathbf{b}, & \text{if } \mathbf{c} - A^\top\boldsymbol\mu - \boldsymbol\nu = \mathbf{0},\\[2pt]
-\infty, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$
The dual problem maximizes $g$ over $\boldsymbol\mu\ge0,\boldsymbol\nu\ge0$. Since $-\infty$
is never the maximizer, the dual *automatically enforces* the coefficient-zero condition as
a constraint, and we can eliminate $\boldsymbol\nu$: $\boldsymbol\nu = \mathbf{c} -
A^\top\boldsymbol\mu$, and $\boldsymbol\nu\ge\mathbf{0}$ becomes $A^\top\boldsymbol\mu\le
\mathbf{c}$. The dual is therefore
$$
\boxed{\ \max_{\boldsymbol\mu\ge 0}\ \mathbf{b}^\top\boldsymbol\mu
\quad\text{subject to}\quad A^\top\boldsymbol\mu \le \mathbf{c}.\ }
$$
This is itself a linear program — the famous **LP dual**. Note the elegant role-swap:
the primal's cost vector $\mathbf{c}$ becomes the dual's constraint bound; the primal's
constraint bound $\mathbf{b}$ becomes the dual's cost; and $A$ transposes. Because all
constraints are affine, **strong duality holds whenever either problem is feasible**, so the
two optimal values coincide. @fig-lp-duality solves a small concrete LP both ways and shows
the dual value rising to meet the primal value.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lp-duality
#| fig-cap: 'A 2-variable LP and its dual, solved numerically. **Left:** the primal feasible polygon (intersection of $A\mathbf{x}\geq\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{x}\geq0$) with cost contours $\mathbf{c}^\top\mathbf{x}$; the minimum sits at a vertex (green). **Right:** the dual feasible region $A^\top\boldsymbol\mu\leq\mathbf{c},\ \boldsymbol\mu\geq0$ with its objective $\mathbf{b}^\top\boldsymbol\mu$ maximized (red). The two optimal values are printed and **match to numerical precision** — strong duality for an LP.'
from scipy.optimize import linprog

# Primal: min c^T x  s.t.  A x >= b,  x >= 0
c = np.array([2.0, 3.0])
A = np.array([[1.0, 1.0],
              [1.0, 2.0]])
b = np.array([4.0, 5.0])

# scipy minimizes with A_ub x <= b_ub; convert A x >= b  ->  -A x <= -b
res_p = linprog(c, A_ub=-A, b_ub=-b, bounds=[(0, None), (0, None)])
p_val = res_p.fun
x_opt = res_p.x

# Dual: max b^T mu  s.t. A^T mu <= c, mu >= 0  ->  min -b^T mu
res_d = linprog(-b, A_ub=A.T, b_ub=c, bounds=[(0, None), (0, None)])
d_val = -res_d.fun
mu_opt = res_d.x

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.4))

# Primal feasible region (x1,x2 >= 0 and Ax >= b)
xx = np.linspace(0, 6, 400)
yy = np.linspace(0, 6, 400)
XX, YY = np.meshgrid(xx, yy)
feas = (A[0,0]*XX + A[0,1]*YY >= b[0]) & (A[1,0]*XX + A[1,1]*YY >= b[1])
axL.contourf(XX, YY, feas.astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[PRIMAL], alpha=0.15)
COST = c[0]*XX + c[1]*YY
axL.contour(XX, YY, COST, levels=10, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.6)
axL.scatter([x_opt[0]], [x_opt[1]], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
            linewidth=1.3, label=fr"primal opt, $p^\star={p_val:.3f}$")
axL.set_xlim(0, 6); axL.set_ylim(0, 6)
axL.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$")
axL.set_title("Primal LP")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)

# Dual feasible region (mu >= 0 and A^T mu <= c)
mm = np.linspace(0, 4, 400)
nn = np.linspace(0, 4, 400)
M1, M2 = np.meshgrid(mm, nn)
AT = A.T
dfeas = (AT[0,0]*M1 + AT[0,1]*M2 <= c[0]) & (AT[1,0]*M1 + AT[1,1]*M2 <= c[1])
axR.contourf(M1, M2, dfeas.astype(float), levels=[0.5, 1.5], colors=[DUAL], alpha=0.15)
DOBJ = b[0]*M1 + b[1]*M2
axR.contour(M1, M2, DOBJ, levels=10, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.6)
axR.scatter([mu_opt[0]], [mu_opt[1]], color=DUAL, s=110, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
            linewidth=1.3, label=fr"dual opt, $d^\star={d_val:.3f}$")
axR.set_xlim(0, 4); axR.set_ylim(0, 4)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\mu_1$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\mu_2$")
axR.set_title("Dual LP")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"primal optimum p* = {p_val:.6f}")
print(f"dual   optimum d* = {d_val:.6f}")
print(f"duality gap p* - d* = {p_val - d_val:.2e}  (== 0 to precision: strong duality)")
```

The printed gap is zero to machine precision: **the dual LP's best lower bound is exactly
the primal LP's cost.** That is the whole content of LP duality, and the simplex method
secretly maintains a primal and a dual solution simultaneously, terminating when their gap
hits zero.

## Example 2 — the support vector machine: where duality unlocks kernels {#sec-svm}

This is the example the Classification track links here for, and the single most important
payoff of duality in machine learning. We give the optimization core; the SVM *model*
(margins, why we maximize them, the classification rule) is owned by Classification, which
links back here for this derivation.

**Primal (hard-margin SVM).** Given linearly separable data $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ with
labels $y_i\in\{-1,+1\}$, find the maximum-margin separating hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top
\mathbf{x}+b=0$. Maximizing the margin is minimizing $\tfrac12\|\mathbf{w}\|^2$ subject to
every point being correctly classified with margin at least one:
$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b}\ \tfrac12\|\mathbf{w}\|^2
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \quad i=1,\dots,N.
$$
This is convex (quadratic objective, affine constraints), so strong duality will hold. Put
the constraints in $g\le 0$ form, $g_i = 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b)\le 0$, with
multipliers $\alpha_i\ge 0$ (one per *training point*):
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol\alpha)
= \tfrac12\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + \sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_i\bigl(1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b)\bigr).
$$

**Minimize over the primal variables $\mathbf{w}, b$.** Set the gradients to zero. For
$\mathbf{w}$, using $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\tfrac12\|\mathbf{w}\|^2=\mathbf{w}$ and
$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}[-\alpha_i y_i\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i]=-\alpha_i y_i\mathbf{x}_i$:
$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i.
$$ {#eq-svm-w}
This is already remarkable: **the optimal weight vector is a linear combination of the
training points**, weighted by $\alpha_i y_i$. For $b$, only the term $-\sum_i\alpha_i y_i b$
depends on $b$, with derivative $-\sum_i\alpha_i y_i$:
$$
\frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_i y_i = 0.
$$ {#eq-svm-b}

**Substitute back to get the dual function.** Plug @eq-svm-w into $\mathcal{L}$ and use
@eq-svm-b to kill the $b$-term. First the norm:
$$
\tfrac12\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 = \tfrac12\Bigl(\sum_i\alpha_i y_i\mathbf{x}_i\Bigr)^\top\Bigl(\sum_j\alpha_j y_j\mathbf{x}_j\Bigr)
= \tfrac12\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j\,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j.
$$
Next the constraint term. Expand $\sum_i\alpha_i(1 - y_i\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i - y_i b)
= \sum_i\alpha_i - \sum_i\alpha_i y_i\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i - b\sum_i\alpha_i y_i$. The
last piece is zero by @eq-svm-b. The middle piece, substituting $\mathbf{w}=\sum_j\alpha_j
y_j\mathbf{x}_j$, is $\sum_i\alpha_i y_i\bigl(\sum_j\alpha_j y_j\mathbf{x}_j\bigr)^\top
\mathbf{x}_i = \sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j\,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$. Combining,
$$
g(\boldsymbol\alpha) = \underbrace{\tfrac12\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j}_{\text{from }\frac12\|w\|^2}
+ \sum_i\alpha_i - \underbrace{\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j}_{\text{from constraint}}
= \sum_i\alpha_i - \tfrac12\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j\,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j.
$$
So the **dual SVM** is
$$
\boxed{\ \max_{\boldsymbol\alpha\ge 0}\ \sum_{i=1}^N \alpha_i
- \tfrac12\sum_{i,j}\alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j\,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j
\quad\text{subject to}\quad \sum_i\alpha_i y_i = 0.\ }
$$ {#eq-svm-dual}
Three consequences, each a gift of duality:

- **The kernel trick lives here.** The dual touches the data **only** through the inner
  products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$. Replace every such inner product with a
  *kernel* $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)$ — an inner product in some richer (possibly
  infinite-dimensional) feature space — and you get a nonlinear classifier *without ever
  computing in that space*. This is impossible to even state from the primal, where
  $\mathbf{w}$ would be an infinite-dimensional object. Duality is what makes kernels
  possible. (The kernel trick itself is owned by [CLAS], introduced with the SVM; here we
  show only *why the dual is the door it walks through*.)
- **Sparsity via complementary slackness.** By @eq-comp-slack, $\alpha_i>0$ only for points
  with $g_i=0$, i.e. points *exactly on the margin* — the **support vectors**. All other
  points have $\alpha_i=0$ and drop out of @eq-svm-w entirely. The classifier depends only
  on the support vectors; this is the SVM's defining frugality, and it is a *derived*
  consequence of duality, not a design choice.
- **The dual is the easier problem.** It is a concave quadratic in $\boldsymbol\alpha$ with
  one simple equality and box constraints $\boldsymbol\alpha\ge0$ — exactly what specialized
  solvers (SMO) chew through efficiently.

@fig-svm fits the dual SVM on a small 2-D dataset and marks the support vectors that
complementary slackness selects.

```{python}
#| label: fig-svm
#| fig-cap: 'The hard-margin SVM, solved through its dual @eq-svm-dual. Two linearly separable classes (blue/red). Solving the dual quadratic program for $\boldsymbol\alpha$ and reconstructing $\mathbf{w}=\sum_i\alpha_i y_i\mathbf{x}_i$ gives the maximum-margin separator (black) and its margins (dashed). Complementary slackness forces $\alpha_i>0$ only on the margin: the circled **support vectors** are the handful of points with nonzero dual weight; every other point could be deleted without changing the classifier.'
from scipy.optimize import minimize

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
n_per = 12
Xpos = rng.normal([2.2, 2.2], 0.55, size=(n_per, 2))
Xneg = rng.normal([-1.0, -1.0], 0.55, size=(n_per, 2))
Xsvm = np.vstack([Xpos, Xneg])
ysvm = np.hstack([np.ones(n_per), -np.ones(n_per)])
N = len(ysvm)

# Dual: max sum a - 0.5 a^T Q a  s.t. sum a_i y_i = 0, a >= 0
# Q_ij = y_i y_j x_i . x_j ; we minimize the negative.
Q = (ysvm[:, None] * ysvm[None, :]) * (Xsvm @ Xsvm.T)

def neg_dual(a):
    return -(a.sum() - 0.5 * a @ Q @ a)
def neg_dual_grad(a):
    return -(np.ones_like(a) - Q @ a)

cons = {"type": "eq", "fun": lambda a: a @ ysvm, "jac": lambda a: ysvm}
bnds = [(0, None)] * N
res = minimize(neg_dual, np.zeros(N), jac=neg_dual_grad, bounds=bnds,
               constraints=cons, method="SLSQP",
               options={"maxiter": 500, "ftol": 1e-10})
alpha = np.maximum(res.x, 0)
w = (alpha * ysvm) @ Xsvm
sv = alpha > 1e-4
# b from a support vector: y_i (w.x_i + b) = 1
b = np.mean(ysvm[sv] - Xsvm[sv] @ w)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 5.2))
ax.scatter(Xpos[:, 0], Xpos[:, 1], color=PRIMAL, s=45, label="class $+1$")
ax.scatter(Xneg[:, 0], Xneg[:, 1], color=DUAL, s=45, label="class $-1$")
ax.scatter(Xsvm[sv, 0], Xsvm[sv, 1], facecolor="none", edgecolor=ACCENT, s=180,
           linewidth=2.2, label="support vectors ($\\alpha_i>0$)")
xline = np.linspace(-2.8, 4.0, 50)
# decision boundary w.x + b = 0  ->  x2 = -(w0 x1 + b)/w1
ax.plot(xline, -(w[0]*xline + b)/w[1], color="black", lw=2.0, label="separator")
ax.plot(xline, -(w[0]*xline + b - 1)/w[1], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.plot(xline, -(w[0]*xline + b + 1)/w[1], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.set_xlim(-2.8, 4.0); ax.set_ylim(-2.8, 4.0)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$")
ax.set_title("SVM via the dual: support vectors carry the solution")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"number of support vectors (alpha>0): {sv.sum()} of {N} points")
print(f"sum_i alpha_i y_i = {alpha @ ysvm: .2e}  (== 0, the dual equality constraint)")
```

The printout confirms both dual facts operationally: only a handful of points have nonzero
$\alpha_i$ (the support vectors), and the dual equality $\sum_i\alpha_i y_i=0$ holds to
precision. We solved a search over $N$ numbers $\alpha_i$ instead of over $\mathbf{w}$, and
in doing so exposed the inner-product structure that kernels exploit.

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: a non-convex problem with a positive gap {#sec-edge}

Every honest treatment shows where the prize *fails to materialize*. Strong duality needs
convexity; without it the gap can be strictly positive, and solving the (easy, convex) dual
gives only a lower bound, not the answer. Here is a clean instance with a wide-open gap.

**Primal.** Minimize a wavy non-convex objective on an interval enforced by one inequality:
$$
\min_{x}\ f(x) = x + 6\cos x
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
g(x) = x^2 - 25 \le 0
\quad(\text{i.e. } -5 \le x \le 5).
$$
The objective is **non-convex** — $f''(x) = -6\cos x$ flips sign repeatedly, so the graph
is a tilted sequence of humps and valleys. On $[-5,5]$ the lowest valley is an interior
local minimum (we locate it numerically below), so the hypothesis of @thm-slater is
violated and we have no guarantee the gap closes.

**Dual.** With multiplier $\mu\ge 0$, the Lagrangian is
$\mathcal{L}(x,\mu)=x+6\cos x+\mu(x^2-25)$. The dual function is
$g(\mu)=\inf_x\mathcal{L}(x,\mu)$, taken over the *whole* line. Two observations make the
dual well-behaved here: for $\mu=0$ the relaxation $x+6\cos x$ runs to $-\infty$ as
$x\to-\infty$ (so $g(0)=-\infty$, a useless price), but for any $\mu>0$ the $\mu x^2$ term
dominates at large $|x|$ and makes the Lagrangian **coercive** (it grows without bound in
both directions), so its infimum is finite and *attained*. The dual is then a finite,
concave function of $\mu$ — exactly as @thm-dual-concave promised, regardless of the
primal's waviness. We compute it on a grid and compare its peak $d^\star$ to the true
constrained optimum $p^\star$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gap
#| fig-cap: 'A strictly positive duality gap. We minimize the non-convex $f(x)=x+6\cos x$ subject to $g(x)=x^2-25\leq0$ (so $-5\leq x\leq5$, shaded). **Left:** the objective is a tilted washboard; the constrained minimum $p^\star$ (green) is the deepest interior valley. **Right:** the dual function $g(\mu)=\min_{x}[f(x)+\mu(x^2-25)]$ over admissible prices $\mu\geq0$. It is concave (the hump of @thm-dual-concave), but its maximum $d^\star$ (red) sits **well below** $p^\star$ (green dashed) — the duality gap is large and unmistakable, because $f$ is non-convex and no toll can make the relaxed minimum reach the constrained one.'
def f_nc(x):
    return x + 6.0 * np.cos(x)
def g_nc(x):
    return x**2 - 25.0

# primal optimum on the feasible interval [-5, 5]
xg = np.linspace(-5.0, 5.0, 40001)
p_star = f_nc(xg).min()
x_pstar = xg[np.argmin(f_nc(xg))]

# dual: for each mu >= 0, minimize the Lagrangian over a wide x-grid (coercive for mu>0)
xfine = np.linspace(-30.0, 30.0, 60001)
mus = np.linspace(0.0, 6.0, 1200)
dvals = np.array([(f_nc(xfine) + m * g_nc(xfine)).min() for m in mus])
d_star = dvals.max()
mu_star = mus[np.argmax(dvals)]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.6, 4.4))
xplot = np.linspace(-6.5, 6.5, 800)
axL.axvspan(-5, 5, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.08, label="feasible $[-5,5]$")
axL.plot(xplot, f_nc(xplot), color=PRIMAL, lw=2.2, label=r"$f(x)=x+6\cos x$")
axL.scatter([x_pstar], [p_star], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
            linewidth=1.3, label=fr"$p^\star={p_star:.3f}$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
axL.set_title("Non-convex primal")
axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)

axR.plot(mus, dvals, color=DUAL, lw=2.2, label=r"dual $g(\mu)$")
axR.axhline(p_star, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.4, label=fr"$p^\star={p_star:.3f}$")
axR.scatter([mu_star], [d_star], color=DUAL, s=90, zorder=6, edgecolor="white",
            linewidth=1.2, label=fr"$d^\star={d_star:.3f}$")
axR.annotate("", xy=(mu_star, p_star), xytext=(mu_star, d_star),
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=GOLD, lw=1.8))
axR.text(mu_star + 0.15, (p_star + d_star)/2, f"gap\n$={p_star-d_star:.3f}$",
         color=GOLD, fontsize=9)
axR.set_xlabel(r"price $\mu \geq 0$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"dual value $g(\mu)$")
axR.set_ylim(d_star - 3, p_star + 3)
axR.set_title("Concave dual, but its max falls short")
axR.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"primal optimum p* = {p_star:.4f}  at x* = {x_pstar:.3f}")
print(f"dual   optimum d* = {d_star:.4f}  at mu* = {mu_star:.3f}")
print(f"duality gap p* - d* = {p_star - d_star:.4f}  (> 0: strong duality FAILS)")
```

The printed gap is strictly positive and large. Notice the dual function is still
**concave** (the right panel is a clean hump, exactly as @thm-dual-concave guarantees
regardless of the primal's non-convexity) — but its peak lands *below* the true optimum.
**This is the precise boundary of duality's power:** the dual is always solvable and always
a valid lower bound, but only convexity (plus Slater) promises the bound is *tight*. For
non-convex problems the gap is the price of the relaxation, and you reach for
branch-and-bound, semidefinite relaxations (SDP, which often tightens the gap dramatically),
or global solvers to close it.

# Failure modes and when to reach for the dual {#sec-failure}

Judgment is the goal; here is the honest map of where duality helps and where it does not.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A small or zero duality gap is a *property*, not a guarantee — verify the conditions.**
The recurring mistakes:

1. **Assuming the gap is zero.** It is zero *for convex problems satisfying a constraint
   qualification* (Slater for inequalities, feasibility for affine ones). For non-convex
   problems (@sec-edge) the gap is generally positive — the dual optimum is a **lower
   bound**, not the answer. Reporting $d^\star$ as "the solution" of a non-convex problem is
   wrong.
2. **Forgetting $\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$.** Weak duality and the lower-bound certificate
   require the inequality multipliers to be non-negative. Drop that and the toll can become a
   bonus for violating constraints, destroying the bound.
3. **Minimizing the Lagrangian jointly.** As [OPTM-13] warned, the saddle is a *minimum in
   $\mathbf{x}$, maximum in the prices*. The dual function minimizes over $\mathbf{x}$ for
   **fixed** prices, then the dual problem *maximizes* over prices. Doing both as a single
   minimization sends the multipliers to $\pm\infty$.
4. **Trusting a $-\infty$ dual value.** At a price where $g(\boldsymbol\lambda,\boldsymbol\mu)=-\infty$,
   the dual certifies nothing — that price says only "$p^\star\ge-\infty$." This happens
   when the relaxed Lagrangian is unbounded below in $\mathbf{x}$, as at $\mu=0$ in
   @sec-edge (the term $x+6\cos x$ runs to $-\infty$ as $x\to-\infty$). The dual *problem*
   simply discards such prices, so the failure is only fatal if it occurs at **every**
   admissible price — i.e. no toll makes the Lagrangian coercive in $\mathbf{x}$, which
   leaves the dual with no finite lower bound to offer at all.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you actually solve the dual instead of the primal?"* Four concrete triggers,
which are the practitioner's whole decision rule:

- **The dual is lower-dimensional or simpler.** SVMs (one variable per data point, only
  inner products appear), LPs where the dual has fewer constraints, problems with many
  inequality constraints but few variables.
- **You need a certificate.** Any feasible dual point bounds the primal optimum, so you can
  *stop early* with a guarantee: if the primal cost of your current iterate and a dual lower
  bound are within $\varepsilon$, you are within $\varepsilon$ of optimal. This **duality
  gap as a stopping criterion** is built into interior-point methods.
- **The constraints carry the structure you want to exploit.** Kernels (SVM), network-flow
  duality, decomposition methods (Lagrangian relaxation / dual decomposition splits a
  coupled problem into independent pieces by pricing the coupling constraints).
- **The primal is non-convex but you want a bound.** The dual gives a cheap, always-valid
  lower bound to seed branch-and-bound or to assess how far a heuristic solution might be
  from optimal.

And the honest counterpoint: *for an unconstrained or simply-constrained convex problem you
can already solve directly (descent, Newton), the dual is extra machinery you do not need.*
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived through the constrained-optimization branch: [OPTM-13] built the Lagrangian and
the shadow-price reading of the multiplier; [OPTM-14] added the inequality machinery — the
sign condition $\boldsymbol\mu\ge\mathbf{0}$ and complementary slackness — as a KKT *test*.
This article promoted the multiplier from a byproduct to the **decision variable of a twin
problem**, proved that twin always lower-bounds the original and is always convex, and
showed that for convex problems the two coincide — at which point the KKT conditions
([OPTM-14]) re-emerge as the *consequence* of strong duality (@sec-kkt-link), not an
axiom. This completes the constrained-optimization theory. From here:

- **[OPTM-16 *Proximal and Projected Methods*]** — the *algorithms* that solve constrained
  and non-smooth problems numerically, several of which (dual ascent, ADMM) operate directly
  on the dual variables this article introduced.
- **The dual SVM and the kernel trick** ([CLAS], support vector machines) — the exact
  derivation of @sec-svm is what Classification links here for; the kernel trick is the
  Classification track's to own, and it is *possible only because the dual exposes inner
  products*.
- **Duality as minimax and game theory** — the saddle structure
  $\max_{\boldsymbol\mu\ge0}\min_{\mathbf{x}}\mathcal{L}$ is von Neumann's minimax; strong
  duality is the statement that the order of min and max can be swapped, the bridge to
  two-player zero-sum games.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The standard reference.** Boyd & Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization*, Chapter 5
  (duality) — the cleanest modern treatment of the dual function, weak/strong duality,
  Slater's condition, the geometric (achievable-set) picture of @sec-strong, and the KKT
  connection. This article follows its framing.
- **Primary threads.** Von Neumann's 1928 minimax theorem and its 1947 connection to LP
  duality (with Morgenstern); Gale, Kuhn, and Tucker's 1951 linear-programming duality;
  Slater's 1950 constraint-qualification note; Rockafellar's *Convex Analysis* (1970) for
  the full convex-analytic machinery (conjugate functions, the dual as a conjugate).
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *The complete proof of strong
  duality* — we gave the separating-hyperplane geometry and imported the full theorem;
  the rigorous version (via the supporting hyperplane to the convex achievable set, or via
  Fenchel conjugates) lives in Boyd & Vandenberghe §5.3 and Rockafellar. *The kernel trick
  itself* — owned by [CLAS], which builds on @sec-svm. *Dual-based algorithms* (dual ascent,
  augmented Lagrangian, ADMM, dual decomposition) — [OPTM-16] and the numerical-optimization
  literature (Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization*). *Conic and semidefinite duality*,
  which tightens the gap for many non-convex problems — a graduate convex-optimization course
  or Boyd & Vandenberghe §5.9 and the SDP literature.
